name: Kodanda
description: Kodanda, meaning 'made of bamboo,' is the celestial bow of Lord Rama in Hindu mythology. It is not merely a weapon but a symbol of dharma, righteousness, and unwavering commitment. Forged by the divine architect Vishwakarma, Kodanda is described as being indestructible and capable of firing arrows that never miss their target. Its sound alone is said to strike fear into the hearts of enemies. More than its physical properties, Kodanda represents Rama's adherence to his vows and his unwavering devotion to justice. The bow's power is amplified by Rama's own divine nature and his mastery of archery. The Kodanda is often depicted as being adorned with jewels and gold, a testament to its divine origin and the prestige it holds. It is a focal point in many narratives surrounding Rama's battles against evil forces, most notably Ravana, the demon king of Lanka. The bow's significance extends beyond its use in warfare, symbolizing the triumph of good over evil and the upholding of moral principles.
subtitle: The celestial bow of Lord Rama.
origin: Forged by Vishwakarma, the divine architect, for Lord Shiva and eventually passed down to Rama.
currentLocation: Lost, believed to have been returned to the divine realm after Rama's earthly departure.

keyMyths.title: Rama's Swayamvara
keyMyths.description: The Kodanda played a pivotal role in Rama's swayamvara (marriage ceremony). The condition for winning Sita's hand was to string the bow of Shiva, a feat no other king or prince could accomplish. Rama, with his divine strength and grace, not only strung the bow but also broke it in the process, thus proving his worthiness and establishing his divine lineage. This event marked the beginning of Rama's journey and his destined role in upholding dharma.

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Lanka
keyMyths.description: During the epic battle of Lanka, Kodanda was instrumental in Rama's victory over Ravana. Rama wielded the bow with unmatched skill, firing arrows that decimated the demon army and ultimately felled Ravana himself. The bow's unfailing accuracy and divine power ensured that each arrow struck its target, contributing to the triumph of good over evil. The Kodanda became a symbol of hope and righteousness in the face of overwhelming odds.

keyMyths.title: Parashurama's Challenge
keyMyths.description: After Rama broke Shiva's bow, Parashurama, an avatar of Vishnu known for his prowess with weapons, challenged Rama to a duel. Parashurama, initially enraged by the breaking of Shiva's bow (a bow given to him by Shiva), eventually recognized Rama's divine nature and conceded defeat. This encounter further solidified Rama's position as an avatar of Vishnu and a worthy wielder of the Kodanda.

sources.author: Valmiki
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Rama and the Kodanda. Provides detailed accounts of its use and significance.

sources.description: A popular retelling of the Ramayana in Awadhi, offering a devotional perspective on Rama and his divine bow.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: A scholarly analysis of Hindu mythology, including the significance of weapons like the Kodanda in shaping narratives and beliefs.
